title: NATO leaders gather in Warsaw amid push for long-term Ukraine funding
author: Luke Harding
body:
WARSAW — NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte opened a two-day summit in Warsaw on Tuesday with a call for allies to commit at least 100 billion euros in multi-year military aid to Ukraine, as Russian forces pressed attacks near Pokrovsk and Kharkiv.

Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy met Rutte and Polish President Andrzej Duda at the Royal Castle, thanking Poland for hosting millions of refugees and for coordinating logistics hubs near Rzeszów. Zelenskyy said Ukraine needed more air defense missiles, artillery shells and training for F-16 pilots operating from bases in Romania and the Baltic states.

U.S. Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth told reporters that Washington would maintain intelligence sharing and maintenance support, but urged European capitals to lead on procurement. German Chancellor Olaf Scholz announced a new ammunition joint venture with Rheinmetall and Leonardo, while UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer confirmed additional Storm Shadow deliveries coordinated with France.

Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Norwegian Prime Minister Jonas Gahr Støre backed a NATO-Ukraine compact that would streamline weapons transfers and cyber defense assistance. Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdoğan offered to renew talks on grain exports through the Black Sea, though he criticized Western sanctions on Russian energy that affect Turkish refineries.

Russian foreign ministry spokesperson Maria Zakharova dismissed the Warsaw meeting as escalatory and accused NATO of fighting a proxy war. Kremlin aide Yuri Ushakov said Moscow remained open to negotiations only if Ukraine accepted neutral status and recognized administrative changes in Donetsk and Crimea.

In Kyiv, energy officials reported drone strikes on substations near Odesa, leaving parts of the city without power. The International Atomic Energy Agency said it was monitoring safety at the Zaporizhzhia Nuclear Power Plant after nearby shelling.

Analysts at the Atlantic Council said the summit would test whether Europe can sustain aid without U.S. budget guarantees. Rutte closed the first session by inviting Sweden and Finland to expand joint exercises in the Arctic and Baltic Sea, citing hybrid threats from Russia and Belarus along the Suwałki corridor.
